title: The Company That Changed Tech Forever
description: In this video, Sandeep breaks down the unbelievable rise of NVIDIA—from three engineers sketching a chip on a napkin at Denny's to building the most important AI company on the planet. You'll learn how they survived near-bankruptcy, invented the GPU, and sparked a trillion-dollar AI boom—plus why their biggest customers might become their biggest threats.
transcript:
If I told you the most important company
in AI started at Denny's, you wouldn't
believe me. But what these three guys
started became one of the most powerful
tech companies on the planet. I welcome
to Nvidia. This is the story of how
Nvidia survived long enough to become
essential. And whether you are building
a company, a career, or just figuring
out what's next, there is a lot in here
for all of us to learn. So let's dive
into it. Three engineers walked into
Denny's and started a company that would
define the future of intelligence. One
of those engineers, Jensen Wong. The
funny thing is, they didn't set out to
build the greatest AI engine of their
time. They just wanted to make video
games look better. Their passion was to
bring cinematic quality graphics to
real-time gameplay. Back then, and I'm
talking about 1993, if you wanted to
make great animation, you needed a lot
of computing power and expensive
workstations from companies like Silicon
Graphics and Sun. An entry-level Indigo
2 at that point started at
$34,000. A fully spec version around
$86,000. Meanwhile, a high-end PC would
cost around $3,000, but it couldn't
render awesome animation easily. In
1993, when Jurassic Park was released,
the world finally saw the power of 3D
graphics rendered on a computer because
those dinosaurs were created on
workstations made by silicon graphics.
So, what our three friends at Denny's
wanted to do was to bring the same power
to PCs. The PC revolution was just
beginning. And even though Windows 95
hadn't launched yet, the tech world was
full of companies making sound cards and
networking cards. Gaming wasn't even
considered a real industry then. But the
founders had a simple hope. If you build
it, they will come. Huge bet, huge risk.
So they sketched a design on a napkin
for a realtime graphics chip. And that's
how Nvidia started. Pancakes, coffee,
and a shared ambition. Fun fact, Jensen
worked as a dishwasher at Denny's when
he was young. And his favorite dish was
the superbird. That's the kind of
simple, humble diner meal that
eventually led to a trillion dollar
enterprise. But then came the biggest
question, funding. Jensen was introduced
to Sequoa Capital. Now, according to

but the VC firm saw something in him and
they invested. It was a $2 million round
with $6 million post money valuation.
Crazy to think that a $3 trillion
company was once valued at 6 million.
There were over 70 graphic startups
fighting to be the first. Remember,
we're talking about a time where Google
or Meta weren't even born yet. Pretty
soon, Microsoft gaming took off and

Quake. Demand for 3D graphics was just

NV1. It was bold, ambitious. At first,

Nvidia and Sega chose to base their

it introduced Direct 3D which evolved
into DirectX later but it was a new

directly using Windows operating system
and of course Direct X used triangles
the most efficient shape for rendering
any 2D or 3D surface and just as
Microsoft PC gaming takes off Nvidia's
chip becomes already obsolete. They sold
250,000 chips and
249,000 came back. The market had spoken
and it wasn't pretty. The biggest lesson
is next. The gamble of honesty. Remember
the first chip that didn't work?
Unfortunately, their next move made
survival even harder. Their follow-up
product NV2 was also incompatible with
industry standards and became obsolete
before it was even released. The only
customer that had signed up was Sega. In
fact, Nvidia was already busy
manufacturing NV2 when Sega told them he
was switching horses and adapting
DirectX. Nvidia had picked its own lane,
but the rest of the world was now
driving in the opposite direction, and
the company had only nine months of
runway left before bankruptcy. Jensen at
that point had two choices. Finish the
Sega contract and run out of cash
slowly, or cancel it and run out of cash

driver's seat in a car without brakes,
staring at a cliff that's approaching
fast. But there's always a third way
hiding beneath the only two ways we
think we have. The Nvidia CEO believed
that there was a third choice. So he
flew to Japan to meet the Sega CEO. He
looked him in the eye and told him the
honest to god truth that NV2 wasn't
going to work for Sega and that Sega
should find another chip partner. As
bold as it sounds, he asked Sega to pay
Nvidia the full contractual amount
anyway because without that money,
Nvidia wasn't going to survive. Some
would call it gutsy, others might call
it honest. Either way, it worked. Nvidia
lived to see another day. Next is one of
my favorite lessons. Sometimes the most
important win is to simply stay alive.
Nvidia laid off 75% of the team down to
just 35 employees. And those who stayed
knew the truth. They had to build a new

months or game over. Normally chip

a very slow, cumbersome process

TSMC. Nvidia did not have that luxury.
So they took a wild gamble. They tested

was like, "Hey guys, are you sure about

was compatible with industry standard,
Nvidia had only enough cash left in the

were 30 days away from going bankrupt.
But once again, the gamble paid off.
Sure enough, the chip was buggy, but it
was also a total beast. Way more
powerful than anything else in the
market. They sold nearly 1 million units
in its first four months. Somehow Nvidia
was saved from the brink once again. So
the team learned two big lessons. One,
how to get the best performance per
dollar. And two, how to ship a new chip
in 6 months when everybody else was
taking 2 years. Sometimes necessity
really is the mother of invention. And
then came 1999.
This is the year when Nvidia launched
GeForce, their first GPU. It wasn't just

A CPU is like a chef cooking a very

$735 million and then doubles again at$
1.4 billion. That's just incredible

CUDA had very little to do with gaming,
which was Nvidia's core market at that

And meanwhile, ATI and AMD were catching

getting tougher. Margins were shrinking.
And then Nvidia missed their earnings.
The stock plummeted by 80%. How do you
recover from something like that? What
most people missed was this. Nvidia was
slowly pivoting away from graphics and
moving toward parallel computing. There

knew it was going to come. In tech, you

Nvidia kind of meandered. growth was

missed again. Stock fell another 50%,
people started writing them off. What
they really needed was a miracle. Next
is when the big bang finally happens. In
2012, a team entered a competition
called ImageNet at the University of
Toronto. Now, the internet competition
is effectively the World Cup for machine
learning and computer vision. Everyone
was given 14 million labeled images with
1,000 object categories, and all the
teams had just one challenge to teach
machines how to see. Now, most teams
used traditional tools and came up with
average results, except for this one
team. They did something completely
different. They trained a deep neural
network called AlexNet. It had about 60
million parameters that was massive at
that time. Processing at that scale
would have taken weeks or even months
for other teams. Alexnet finished it in
days. And they didn't just win, they
shattered all performance benchmarks and
literally pulled the future forward. And
what made this tech miracle possible?
two GPU cards made by, you guessed it,
Nvidia. This moment became a turning
point for the entire industry and GPUs
were now the future of all AI. If every
other team in that competition was
driving a Prius, Alexet showed up in an
F1 car with jet boosters. Suddenly, deep
learning and high performance computing
was everywhere. And it didn't just shake
the tech world. It's something that
ended up touching all of us. AI wasn't
just a research project. It was
something that hundreds of millions of
us started using every day. AI had moved
from recognizing images and words to
generating them. Of course, Jensen did
not plan Alexet. He did not see it
coming. It was indeed the miracle Nvidia
needed. And sometimes I think you have
to survive long enough to catch the
right wave. Nvidia now held the keys to
the most valuable gates in tech. Gates
that could open a new $10 trillion
market. But Nvidia didn't slow down.
They made three bets, each bigger than
the last. The Big Bang happened and
there was no time to celebrate. What
happened in the next 5 years defied
gravity. Jensen accelerated the pace of
innovation like no one else. They made
three decisive strategic plays. First,
the tensor core architecture. Volulta
was their first product that introduced
tensor cores. Now, that's an
architecture built to accelerate matrix
math and that's the language of neural
networks and deep learning. Then came
Mier, then Hopper. Second, Nvidia
assembled all the AI capabilities in a
box. It was their DGX line. Complete AI

AI models. And one of those AI boxes, it

that built Chat GPT. And third, Nvidia
embedded itself into every major cloud
provider. Amazon, Microsoft, Google.
Nvidia positioned itself as the AI
platform of choice. From image
recognition to self-driving cars, from
Hollywood effects to social media feeds,
from ad targeting to crypto mining, all
the way to models like Chat GPT and
Gemini. All that hustle, all that speed,
all that innovation, it paid off.
Nvidia's revenue went from $27 billion
to $61 billion in one year, and the next
year it crossed 130 billion. Everyone
says Nvidia is unstoppable. But are
they? Here's the truth about Nvidia that
most of us don't realize. That the
hardest climb is yet to come. Nvidia is
one of the most valuable companies in
the world today and that also means that
the investor expectations are skyhigh.
Today, Nvidia has come a long way from
the graphics cards days. It's not just

entire machine learning stack,
architecture, systems, data centers,
hardware, CUDA, SDKs, drivers, all of it

one, Nvidia's largest customers can't

silicone, their own homegrown chips.
Google has TPUs. Amazon's got tranium.
Microsoft is building Maya. And these
aren't some side projects or
experiments. These are billiondoll
moonshots built by companies with a lot

a real risk. And we have to recognize
that recreating that stack is a massive
challenge. You're talking about
chipto-chip networking, rack level

software libraries is all insanely
capital inensive. Threat number two,
geopolitical fracture. The US government

So, customers have raised a lot of

But they stayed in the ring long enough

after they started. So in your careers
and ventures, it's likely that you will

But as this case study shows, true
belief beats true brilliance, right? We

have to stay standing for the next one.
And please, I am in no way glorifying

Whatever definition of success you have
in mind, please remember that success is

And like Jensen did with Sega, sometimes
